title: Introduction to Computer Graphics
description: Overview of computer graphics, applications, and basic video display devices including Cathode Ray Tubes (CRT), Raster-Scan, and Random-Scan displays.

keyDetails.title: Key Applications
keyDetails.content: User Interfaces (GUI), Presentation Graphics, Computer Art, Entertainment (Movies/Games), Education & Training, Scientific Visualization, Image Processing, and Computer-Aided Design (CAD).

keyDetails.title: CRT Working Principle
keyDetails.content: An electron gun emits an electron beam through focusing and deflection systems to strike a phosphor-coated screen, creating light. The refresh rate (typically 60-80 Hz) keeps the image from flickering.

keyDetails.title: Raster-Scan vs. Random-Scan
keyDetails.content: Raster-Scan sweeps the electron beam row by row from top to bottom, turning the beam on/off to draw pixels based on a frame buffer. Random-Scan (vector/stroke-writing) directs the electron beam only to the parts of the screen where lines are to be drawn, yielding smooth lines but struggling with complex solid shapes.

title: Graphics Software and Polygon Filling
description: Introduction to OpenGL primitives and filling algorithms like Scan-Line, Boundary-Fill, and Flood-Fill with 4/8-connected logic.

keyDetails.title: OpenGL Primitives
keyDetails.content: OpenGL uses vertex coordinates to define shape primitives: GL_POINTS, GL_LINES, GL_LINE_STRIP (connected lines), GL_LINE_LOOP (closed loop), GL_TRIANGLES, GL_QUADS, and GL_POLYGON (general convex filled polygon).

keyDetails.title: Boundary-Fill Algorithm
keyDetails.content: A recursive algorithm that starts at an interior 'seed' point and colors outward pixel-by-pixel until a boundary color is encountered. Standard code checks: if pixel color is not the border color AND not the fill color, paint it and recurse.

keyDetails.title: Flood-Fill Algorithm
keyDetails.content: Similar to Boundary-Fill, but replaces an entire connected region of a specific 'interior' color with a new color. Recurses outward checking if the current pixel is the target old color.

keyDetails.title: 4-Connected vs. 8-Connected
keyDetails.content: 4-Connected checks only immediate horizontal and vertical neighbors (left, right, up, down). 8-Connected checks horizontal, vertical, and diagonal neighbors. 8-Connected fill is required to fill regions with diagonal boundaries to prevent colors leaking through small diagonal gaps.

title: Graphics Hardware
description: Deep dive into raster-scan system architecture, video controllers, display processors, and flat panel technology.

keyDetails.title: Frame Buffer
keyDetails.content: A dedicated memory area storing the intensity/color value of each pixel on the screen. Resolution determines the number of pixels. Color depth (bits per pixel) determines available colors: 1-bit is black & white, 8-bit is 256 colors, 24-bit is true color (16.7 million colors).

keyDetails.title: Video Controller
keyDetails.content: A hardware component that continuously reads the frame buffer at the refresh rate (e.g. 60 Hz) and outputs the analog signals (or digital) to control the display deflections and beam intensities.

keyDetails.title: Display Processor
keyDetails.content: Also called a graphics card/GPU. It offloads graphics commands from the CPU, performing rasterization (converting geometric shapes into frame buffer pixels) and managing display coordinate translations.

title: Design of Line Algorithms
description: Analysis of line rendering algorithms, comparing the Direct Scan, Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA), and Bresenham's line algorithm.

keyDetails.title: Direct Scan Conversion
keyDetails.content: Calculates y = mx + c for each step. Highly inefficient because it requires floating-point multiplication and rounding for every single pixel plotted.

keyDetails.title: DDA Line Algorithm
keyDetails.content: An incremental algorithm. Calculates dy and dx. If slope |m| <= 1, step x by 1 and increment y by m (y_new = y_old + m). If |m| > 1, step y by 1 and increment x by 1/m. Eliminates multiplication, but still requires slow floating-point addition and rounding on each step.

keyDetails.title: Bresenham's Line Algorithm
keyDetails.content: An efficient algorithm that uses only integer addition and subtraction. It calculates a decision parameter P_k at each step to determine whether to increment the other coordinate. For slope 0 <= m <= 1: Start parameter P_0 = 2dy - dx. At each step, if P_k < 0, plot (x+1, y) and P_{k+1} = P_k + 2dy. If P_k >= 0, plot (x+1, y+1) and P_{k+1} = P_k + 2dy - 2dx.

title: Design of Circle Algorithms
description: Derivation and execution of the Midpoint Circle Drawing algorithm using 8-way coordinate symmetry.

keyDetails.title: 8-Way Symmetry
keyDetails.content: A circle is symmetrical about the axes and diagonals. For any calculated point (x, y) in the first octant (x from 0 to y=r/sqrt(2)), we can mirror it to obtain 7 other points: (y, x), (-y, x), (-x, y), (-x, -y), (-y, -x), (y, -x), and (x, -y). This saves 87.5% of computation!

keyDetails.title: Midpoint Circle Algorithm
keyDetails.content: Calculates pixels along a circle perimeter by selecting between the pixel closer to the inside or outside of the mathematical boundary. Initial decision parameter P_0 = 5/4 - r (approximated as 1 - r for integers). At each step: If P_k < 0, select pixel (x+1, y) and P_{k+1} = P_k + 2x + 3. If P_k >= 0, select pixel (x+1, y-1) and P_{k+1} = P_k + 2x - 2y + 5.

title: Design of Ellipse Algorithms
description: Exploring Midpoint Ellipse Drawing algorithm, region boundaries, and 4-way symmetry mapping.

keyDetails.title: 4-Way Symmetry
keyDetails.content: Unlike circles, ellipses only have 4-way symmetry across the quadrant axes. A point (x, y) on the ellipse centered at (0,0) yields points (-x, y), (x, -y), and (-x, -y).

keyDetails.title: Region 1 vs Region 2
keyDetails.content: The ellipse slope changes from 0 to infinity. We divide the first quadrant into Region 1 (where tangent slope > -1, moving faster in x) and Region 2 (where tangent slope <= -1, moving faster in y). The boundary between them occurs when 2 * ry^2 * x >= 2 * rx^2 * y.

keyDetails.title: Midpoint Ellipse Algorithm Steps
keyDetails.content: Region 1 initial parameter: p1_0 = ry^2 - rx^2 * ry + 1/4 * rx^2. In Region 1: if p1_k < 0, next is (x+1, y) and update p1_{k+1}. Else, next is (x+1, y-1). Transition to Region 2 when 2*ry^2*x >= 2*rx^2*y. Region 2 initial parameter: p2_0 = ry^2*(x_last + 0.5)^2 + rx^2*(y_last - 1)^2 - rx^2*ry^2. In Region 2: if p2_k > 0, next is (x, y-1). Else, next is (x+1, y-1).

title: Spline Curves
description: Understanding parametric curves, Bézier curves, B-Splines, and mathematical continuity (parametric and geometric).

keyDetails.title: Spline Definition
keyDetails.content: A spline is a flexible curve that passes through a set of control points. Interpolation curves pass exactly through all control points. Approximation curves trace near the points to outline a smooth shape.

keyDetails.title: Continuity Types
keyDetails.content: Parametric continuity: C^0 means curves join; C^1 means tangent vectors are equal at the joint (smooth transition); C^2 means acceleration/curvature is equal. Geometric continuity: G^0 means joint match; G^1 means tangent vectors point in same direction but have different magnitudes; G^2 means curvatures match proportionally.

keyDetails.title: Bézier Curves
keyDetails.content: Defined by a set of control points P_0 to P_n. The curve is formulated as P(t) = Sum_{i=0}^n B_{i,n}(t) * P_i, where B_{i,n}(t) are Bernstein Polynomials. Key properties: always lies inside the convex hull of control points, starts at P_0 and ends at P_n, and is affine-invariant.

title: Basic 2D Transformations
description: Introduction to spatial transformations: Translation, Scaling, Rotation, and the Homogeneous Coordinates system.

keyDetails.title: Basic Equations
keyDetails.content: Translation: x' = x + Tx, y' = y + Ty. Scaling: x' = x * Sx, y' = y * Sy. Rotation: x' = x * cos(O) - y * sin(O), y' = x * sin(O) + y * cos(O).

keyDetails.title: Homogeneous Coordinates
keyDetails.content: By adding a third dummy coordinate (h=1), 2D points are represented as [x, y, 1]. This turns translation (originally addition) into a matrix multiplication, allowing all transformations to be represented uniformly as 3x3 matrices and combined by matrix multiplication.

keyDetails.title: Matrix Representations
keyDetails.content: Translation matrix: [[1, 0, Tx], [0, 1, Ty], [0, 0, 1]]. Scaling matrix: [[Sx, 0, 0], [0, Sy, 0], [0, 0, 1]]. Rotation matrix: [[cos(O), -sin(O), 0], [sin(O), cos(O), 0], [0, 0, 1]].

title: Composite 2D Transformations
description: Combining multiple 2D transformations: Rotation/Scaling about arbitrary points, Reflections, and Shear transformations.
concepts: Rotation about an Arbitrary Pivot Point
Scaling relative to an Arbitrary Fixed Point

keyDetails.title: Arbitrary Pivot Rotation
keyDetails.content: To rotate an object around any point (Xr, Yr) instead of the origin: 1. Translate the object so (Xr, Yr) matches the origin. 2. Perform rotation around origin. 3. Translate the object back to (Xr, Yr). Formulated as M = T(Xr, Yr) * R(O) * T(-Xr, -Yr).

keyDetails.title: Reflection & Shear
keyDetails.content: Reflection creates a mirror image across an axis (e.g. flipping x-sign reflects over y-axis). Shear distorts shape along an axis: X-shear shifts x-value proportionally to y (x' = x + Shx * y, y' = y).

title: 3D Transformations
description: Extending coordinates to 3D. Matrix representations for 3D Translation, Scaling, and Rotation around coordinate and arbitrary axes.
concepts: 3D Rotations (X-axis, Y-axis, Z-axis, and Arbitrary axis)

keyDetails.title: 3D Coordinates
keyDetails.content: Points in 3D are represented by 4D vectors [x, y, z, 1] using homogeneous coordinates. Transformations are represented by 4x4 matrices.

keyDetails.title: 3D Rotations
keyDetails.content: Rotations around standard axes: Z-axis rotation is identical to 2D rotation in the XY plane. X-axis and Y-axis rotations are cyclic permutations. Rotation around an arbitrary 3D line involves translating the line to the origin, rotating it to align with a coordinate axis (using Euler angles), performing the rotation, and reversing all steps.
